_meta.authoringNotes: Jackson and Feagin 1973 classification (PMID 4351812) for quadriceps contusion severity. Munich consensus 2013 (PMID 23080315) addresses contusion subtypes. Myositis ossificans data per Walczak 2015 (PMID 26133443). PEACE & LOVE acute care (Dubois 2020 PMID 31377722). Beiner & Jokl 2002 Clin Orthop Relat Res review.

h1Title: Muscle Contusion: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Muscle contusion is a direct-blow soft-tissue injury producing bleeding within the muscle and surrounding fascia, most often involving the quadriceps in contact sports such as American football, rugby, soccer, ice hockey, and basketball. It accounts for roughly 8-12% of all musculoskeletal injuries in contact sports and is the leading cause of 'dead leg' incidents. Severity is graded by passive knee flexion 12-24 hours after injury (Jackson and Feagin): mild (over 90 degrees, return-to-play 2-7 days), moderate (45-90 degrees, return 7-21 days), or severe (under 45 degrees, return 21-42+ days). Most contusions heal completely with early protective range-of-motion at 120 degrees of knee flexion, ice, compression, elevation, paracetamol for pain control, and graded rehabilitation. The main long-term complication, myositis ossificans, occurs in 9-17% of severe quadriceps contusions and presents as palpable hardening with restricted motion 2-4 weeks later, confirmed on X-ray or ultrasound.

keyStats.prevalence: 8-12% of all musculoskeletal injuries in contact sports; among the top three soft-tissue injuries in American football and rugby
keyStats.demographics: Male:female ratio approximately 3:1 in elite collision sport; risk depends on sport rather than sex
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence age 15-30 years among contact-sport athletes
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of millions of injuries per year worldwide across recreational and professional sport

definition: Muscle contusion (ICD-10: S70-S79 contusion codes by site) is a closed soft-tissue injury caused by direct compressive trauma — a blow from an opponent's knee, helmet, ball, or piece of equipment — that crushes muscle fibres and capillaries against underlying bone. The injury produces an intramuscular or intermuscular haematoma depending on whether the surrounding fascia stays intact. Intramuscular haematomas remain contained, raise intracompartmental pressure, and cause more pain and longer recovery; intermuscular haematomas dissect along fascial planes and present with more obvious tracking bruising but less pain. The Munich consensus classification places contusion among the structural injuries (Type 3a/3b for partial structural injury) distinct from strains, which involve indirect tensile loading. Jackson and Feagin's 1973 classification grades quadriceps contusion severity by passive knee flexion 12-24 hours after injury: mild (over 90 degrees), moderate (45-90 degrees), severe (under 45 degrees). The dominant complication is myositis ossificans traumatica, a benign heterotopic bone formation within the haematoma that can mature into a stable mass over 3-6 months. Most contusions are managed by sports medicine, primary care, and physiotherapy; orthopaedic referral is needed for severe injuries, suspected compartment syndrome, or large symptomatic myositis ossificans.

typesClassification.description: Localised tenderness, minimal swelling, passive knee flexion above 90 degrees at 12-24 hours, normal gait. Return-to-play 2-7 days.
Swelling, tenderness, antalgic gait, passive knee flexion 45-90 degrees at 12-24 hours. Return-to-play 7-21 days.
Marked swelling, severe pain, unable to weight-bear, passive knee flexion under 45 degrees. Return-to-play 21-42+ days; high risk of myositis ossificans.

Haematoma contained within an intact fascial envelope. Higher intracompartmental pressure, more pain, slower resorption, and higher risk of complications including compartment syndrome and myositis ossificans.
Haematoma tracking between fascial compartments, with more obvious distal bruising but less pain. Lower complication rate; resolves faster than intramuscular variants.

typesClassification.type: Complicated contusion (compartment syndrome or myositis ossificans)
typesClassification.description: Rare emergencies (compartment syndrome) or sub-acute complications (myositis ossificans) that require imaging and sometimes surgery.

primarySymptoms: Localised pain and tenderness over the site of the direct blow, immediately after impact.
Swelling and palpable firmness or fullness in the muscle, developing within hours.
Bruising (ecchymosis) appearing 24-72 hours after the injury, often tracking distally with gravity.
Reduced range of motion of the adjacent joint — knee flexion for quadriceps contusion, shoulder elevation for biceps contusion.
Antalgic gait with shortened stance phase and altered limb mechanics.
Painful contraction of the affected muscle, especially against resistance.
Warmth over the contused area in the first 48 hours.
Late development of a hard, tender lump 2-4 weeks after injury — possible myositis ossificans, particularly after severe quadriceps contusion.
earlyWarningSigns: Severe pain disproportionate to the apparent severity of the blow
Numbness or paraesthesia in the distribution of nearby peripheral nerves
Tense, shiny, painful swelling of the affected compartment
Reduction in passive range of motion of the adjacent joint over the first 24 hours
Worsening rather than improving symptoms 48-72 hours after injury
emergencySigns: Severe pain out of proportion to clinical findings, especially with passive stretch — suggests acute compartment syndrome
Pallor, paraesthesia, pulselessness, or paralysis in the limb distal to the injury
Inability to dorsiflex or evert the foot, or to extend the knee, suggesting deep haematoma compressing nerves
Skin necrosis, blistering, or non-blanching dusky skin over a tense muscle compartment
Severe trauma with associated long-bone fracture, joint dislocation, or open wound

causes.description: Knee-to-thigh impacts in American football, rugby, hockey, soccer, and martial arts produce most lower-limb contusions. Helmet, elbow, and ball impacts cause upper-limb and chest wall contusions. Mechanism distinguishes contusion from strain.

Falls onto hard surfaces, falls from ladders, and being struck by tools or machinery produce contusions in non-sport settings. Older adults are particularly prone to muscle contusions during falls because of reduced subcutaneous padding.

causes.cause: Motor vehicle collisions and pedestrian impacts
causes.description: Direct contact with dashboards, doors, or other vehicle parts during a collision, and pedestrian impacts with vehicles, produce muscle contusions in addition to fractures and visceral injuries.

Boxing, mixed martial arts, kickboxing, and assault produce repeated muscle contusions, particularly of the thighs, abdomen, and chest wall. Cumulative contusions are linked to chronic muscle fibrosis.
Missing or worn-out thigh pads, shin guards, mouth guards, or shoulder pads doubles the risk of muscle contusions in contact sports.

riskFactors.factor: Participation in contact sport (American football, rugby, ice hockey, soccer, lacrosse)
riskFactors.description: Direct contact sports carry incidence of 2-6 per 1,000 player exposure hours for muscle contusions. Non-contact sports have much lower rates.
Properly fitted thigh pads, shin guards, and other protective gear reduce contusion incidence by 30-50%. Worn or missing pads double the risk.
Positions involving frequent high-velocity collisions (running back, linebacker in American football; flanker, prop in rugby) have the highest incidence of severe contusions.

riskFactors.factor: Prior contusion at the same site
riskFactors.description: Recurrent contusion at the same site is associated with chronic muscle fibrosis, persistent pain, and higher risk of myositis ossificans.
Patients on warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, or dual antiplatelet therapy develop larger haematomas after blunt trauma and have higher rates of compartment syndrome and slow recovery.

riskFactors.factor: Bleeding disorders (haemophilia, von Willebrand disease, platelet disorders)
riskFactors.description: Increase haematoma size and recovery time. Patients with haemophilia A or B should receive factor concentrates promptly after significant muscle trauma.

riskFactors.factor: Older age and reduced subcutaneous fat
riskFactors.description: Older adults with reduced soft-tissue padding sustain larger muscle contusions from similar-magnitude impacts.

riskFactors.factor: Acute training load spikes and fatigue
riskFactors.description: Fatigued athletes have slower reaction times and worse positioning, increasing exposure to direct contact in contact sports.

affectedDemographics: Contact sport athletes aged 15-30 sustain the majority of muscle contusions worldwide.
American football, rugby, ice hockey, soccer, and lacrosse: incidence 2-6 per 1,000 player exposure hours; quadriceps and gluteal muscles most affected.
Military personnel and combat athletes have elevated rates of contusion across multiple sites.
Older adults sustain contusions during falls, particularly of the thigh and trunk.
Patients on anticoagulants or with bleeding disorders develop disproportionately large haematomas after minor blows.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical in most cases. History pinpoints the direct-impact mechanism (knee, elbow, ball, helmet, fall, vehicle), the location and force of impact, and any subsequent activity. Examination assesses local tenderness, swelling, palpable haematoma or firmness, range of motion (especially passive knee flexion for quadriceps), strength on resisted contraction, distal pulses, and peripheral nerve function. Quadriceps contusions are graded by Jackson and Feagin at 12-24 hours using passive knee flexion. Imaging is not routinely needed for mild and moderate injuries but is recommended for severe injuries, suspected compartment syndrome, suspected fracture, abnormal neurovascular examination, or unexplained delayed swelling. Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality and identifies intramuscular or intermuscular haematoma, with intramuscular collections appearing hypoechoic acutely and gradually echogenic as they organise. MRI gives the most detailed assessment of injury extent and is used in elite athletes and when myositis ossificans is suspected. Plain radiographs at 2-4 weeks identify mature heterotopic bone in myositis ossificans, although early calcification can be detected by ultrasound from week 2. Suspected acute compartment syndrome warrants urgent compartment pressure measurement (intracompartmental pressure within 30 mmHg of diastolic blood pressure or absolute pressure above 30 mmHg in the affected compartment is diagnostic). Patients on anticoagulants, with large haematomas, or with bleeding disorders require coagulation screening, full blood count, and consideration of imaging-guided drainage in selected cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis and severity (mild, moderate, severe) at 12-24 hours, guiding return-to-play timing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sports medicine doctor or physiotherapist examines tenderness, swelling, and passive knee flexion. Takes 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies intramuscular or intermuscular haematoma, monitors resolution, and detects early myositis ossificans
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies in a comfortable position; sonographer applies gel and scans the muscle. 15-20 minutes. Operator dependent.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detailed assessment for severe injuries, elite athletes, or suspected complications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies still in the MRI scanner for 20-30 minutes. No radiation. Report within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiograph (2-4 weeks after injury, or earlier if fracture suspected)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies myositis ossificans mature bone and excludes fracture or avulsion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard 2-view radiographs, 5-10 minutes. Report same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms acute compartment syndrome when clinical suspicion is high
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialist needle measurement of intracompartmental pressure; takes 5-10 minutes; uncomfortable but tolerable with local anaesthesia.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, coagulation screen, anticoagulant levels
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bleeding disorders or anticoagulant overdose in unexplained large haematoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within hours to 24 hours.

treatmentOverview: Initial management follows the PEACE & LOVE framework: Protect with relative rest in the first 1-3 days, Elevate the limb, Avoid NSAIDs in the very acute phase, Compress with elastic wraps, and Educate the athlete; then Load with progressive activity, Optimism, Vascularisation through cardiovascular exercise that does not stress the injured muscle, and Exercise to restore strength and range of motion. For quadriceps contusion specifically, immediate immobilisation in 120 degrees of knee flexion for the first 24 hours (using an ace wrap or knee brace) has been shown to limit haematoma size and accelerate return-to-play by approximately 4-6 days compared with extension positioning (Aronen et al. 2006). After 24 hours, gentle pain-free active and passive range-of-motion exercises restore knee flexion before any strengthening work. Cryotherapy 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours for the first 48 hours reduces pain and bleeding. Paracetamol up to 4 g/day is the preferred analgesic; NSAIDs are best avoided in the first 3-5 days because they may impair muscle regeneration. Once pain-free active range of motion is achieved, progressive concentric, eccentric, and functional strengthening is introduced, followed by sport-specific drills and return-to-play. Persistent pain, palpable hardening, or restricted motion at 2-4 weeks warrants imaging to exclude myositis ossificans. Acute compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy within 6 hours. Large symptomatic intramuscular haematomas occasionally require ultrasound-guided aspiration. Established myositis ossificans is treated conservatively with indomethacin or bisphosphonates and graded rehabilitation; surgical excision is reserved for mature (over 6 months) symptomatic lesions causing functional limitation, with reassuring imaging confirmation of full skeletal maturity to reduce recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: PEACE & LOVE acute and subacute care
medicalTreatments.description: Protect, Elevate, Avoid NSAIDs, Compress, Educate (days 0-3); then Load, Optimism, Vascularisation, Exercise (day 3 onwards). Replaces older RICE / PRICE protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves time to functional milestones in observational cohorts; current sports medicine consensus

medicalTreatments.name: Knee flexion immobilisation at 120 degrees for first 24 hours (quadriceps contusion)
medicalTreatments.description: Wrap or brace in deep knee flexion immediately after the injury for 24 hours. Reduces haematoma size and accelerates return-to-play by 4-6 days compared with extension positioning.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Aronen et al. 2006: return-to-play 13 days vs 22 days with extension positioning in military cohort

medicalTreatments.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen) 500-1,000 mg up to four times daily
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesia. Avoid NSAIDs in the first 3-5 days. Add short courses of NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) only if paracetamol is inadequate after the acute phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate analgesia for most muscle contusions

medicalTreatments.name: Cryotherapy (ice packs or cold compression devices)
medicalTreatments.description: Ice for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours for the first 48 hours to reduce pain, swelling, and intramuscular bleeding. Cold compression devices may be more effective than ice alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic pain relief and reduced swelling; modest effect on long-term recovery

medicalTreatments.name: Progressive physiotherapy programme
medicalTreatments.description: Restore pain-free range of motion in week 1, build strength in weeks 2-3, return to sport-specific drills in weeks 3-4 (mild-moderate) or weeks 4-8 (severe). Includes hip and core stability work.

medicalTreatments.name: Indomethacin or selective COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) for prevention of myositis ossificans
medicalTreatments.description: Considered in severe quadriceps contusion or after surgery for high-risk patients. Indomethacin 25 mg three times daily or celecoxib 200 mg twice daily for 7-14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence stronger in heterotopic ossification after hip surgery; supportive in muscle contusion case series

medicalTreatments.name: Bisphosphonates (etidronate) for established myositis ossificans
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label use to slow progression and reduce pain in symptomatic myositis ossificans. Limited evidence; usually combined with physiotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Variable; may slow growth and reduce pain in selected cases

surgicalOptions.name: Fasciotomy for acute compartment syndrome
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical emergency. Open release of all compartments of the affected limb within 6 hours of symptom onset to prevent muscle necrosis, nerve injury, and limb loss. Wound is left open initially.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Limb salvage above 90% when performed within 6 hours; significant disability when delayed beyond 12-24 hours

surgicalOptions.name: Ultrasound-guided aspiration of large intramuscular haematoma
surgicalOptions.description: Considered for large painful intramuscular collections (over 50-100 mL) causing significant pain or restricting motion. Performed under sterile conditions with a wide-bore needle.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 60-80% of selected cases; small risk of infection and recurrent collection

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision of mature myositis ossificans
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for symptomatic mature lesions (over 6 months, with cortex and trabecular pattern on imaging) causing functional impairment. Recurrence rate is much lower when the lesion is fully mature at surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 70-85% of selected patients; recurrence under 10% with mature lesions

alternativeTreatments.name: Compression garments
alternativeTreatments.description: Elastic compression sleeves or shorts may reduce swelling and discomfort in the early phase. Continue throughout rehabilitation if tolerated.

alternativeTreatments.name: Heat therapy (after 72 hours)
alternativeTreatments.description: Warm compresses or heat packs applied after the acute inflammatory phase (3-5 days onwards) may improve range of motion and tissue relaxation alongside active exercise.

whySeeSpecialist: Most muscle contusions are managed by primary care and physiotherapy. Sports medicine or orthopaedic referral is needed for severe contusions, suspected compartment syndrome, persistent symptoms beyond 4 weeks, suspected myositis ossificans, large painful haematomas, anticoagulated or bleeding-disorder patients, and elite athletes requiring rapid return-to-play planning.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a sports medicine physician or musculoskeletal physiotherapist familiar with the Jackson-Feagin grading and PEACE & LOVE acute care. For severe contusions, look for clinics with ultrasound imaging in-house and orthopaedic backup for compartment syndrome and surgical aspiration. Patients on anticoagulants benefit from haematology liaison.
hospitalCriteria: Orthopaedic surgery service for fasciotomy and complex cases
Haematology liaison for anticoagulated patients and bleeding disorders
Compartment pressure measurement availability in emergency setting
keyFacilities: Imaging departments with ultrasound and MRI
preventionStrategies: Wear properly fitted protective equipment (thigh pads, shin guards, shoulder pads, mouth guards) for every training session and match.
Maintain conditioning, agility, and reaction time to avoid high-velocity collisions.
Use shielding tactics and proper body positioning during contact (the 'shoulder roll' in rugby, sliding mechanics in soccer).
Replace worn or damaged protective equipment promptly.
Review safety rules and enforce penalties for high tackles and dangerous play.
Manage anticoagulant therapy carefully in athletes who continue contact activity (often by switching to non-contact sport).
lifestyleModifications: Follow the prescribed rehabilitation programme — premature return doubles re-injury risk.
Use compression shorts or wraps in early phases of return-to-play.
Apply ice in the first 48 hours and heat after 3-5 days.
Maintain good sleep (7-9 hours) and nutrition to support recovery.
Avoid alcohol and smoking which impair muscle healing.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.6-2.2 g/kg/day in athletes) for muscle repair
Anti-inflammatory whole-food diet with vegetables, fruits, oily fish, and olive oil
Tart cherry juice and polyphenol-rich foods which may reduce muscle soreness
Adequate hydration (35-40 mL/kg/day plus exercise replacement)
avoid: Excess alcohol which impairs muscle protein synthesis
Routine high-dose NSAID use in the first 3-5 days post injury
Severely energy-restricted diets which slow tissue repair
Smoking and nicotine which reduce microvascular healing

exerciseGuidelines: Immobilise quadriceps contusion in 120 degrees of knee flexion for the first 24 hours, then start pain-free active and passive range-of-motion exercises. Add isometric and gentle concentric strength work in week 1-2, eccentric work and functional drills in week 2-3, and sport-specific running and change-of-direction work in weeks 3-4 (mild-moderate) or weeks 4-8 (severe). Maintain general cardiovascular fitness with cycling, swimming, or upper-body ergometry during recovery.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed range-of-motion and strengthening exercises twice daily.
Apply ice 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours during the first 48 hours.
Use compression shorts or wraps during the early phase.
Avoid NSAIDs in the first 3-5 days; use paracetamol for pain.
Use heat therapy and gentle stretching after 3-5 days.
Track passive knee flexion (or relevant joint range) daily to monitor progress.
prognosis: Most muscle contusions recover fully with appropriate early care. Mild contusions return to sport within 2-7 days, moderate within 7-21 days, and severe within 21-42 days. With early knee flexion immobilisation and structured rehabilitation, severe quadriceps contusions return to play 4-6 days faster than with extension positioning (Aronen 2006). The main long-term complication is myositis ossificans traumatica, which develops in 9-17% of severe quadriceps contusions and 5-8% overall. Most myositis ossificans lesions mature and become asymptomatic over 6-12 months; only 10-20% require surgical excision after full maturation. Acute compartment syndrome is rare but limb-threatening; outcomes are excellent with fasciotomy within 6 hours and poor with delays beyond 12 hours. Recurrent contusion at the same site is associated with chronic muscle fibrosis and residual strength deficit. Older adults and patients on anticoagulants may have prolonged recovery and larger haematomas. Compliance with rehabilitation and protective equipment are the strongest modifiable predictors of good outcome.
recoveryTimeline: Mild contusion: 2-7 days return to play. Moderate: 7-21 days. Severe: 21-42+ days, especially with myositis ossificans. Myositis ossificans usually matures over 3-6 months; surgical excision (if needed) is delayed until lesion is fully mature. Compartment syndrome: surgical emergency with recovery dependent on speed of fasciotomy.
complications: Myositis ossificans traumatica (heterotopic bone formation in muscle) in 9-17% of severe quadriceps contusions — palpable lump and reduced range of motion 2-4 weeks post-injury, confirmed on X-ray or ultrasound.
Acute compartment syndrome — rare but limb-threatening; suspect with severe pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia, and tense compartments; requires fasciotomy within 6 hours.
Persistent intramuscular haematoma needing aspiration or evacuation in 5-10% of large severe contusions.
Chronic muscle fibrosis with strength and flexibility deficit, particularly after recurrent contusion at the same site.
Peripheral nerve injury (e.g., femoral, sciatic, common peroneal) by compression from haematoma.
Rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria and acute kidney injury after extensive crush injury — measure creatine kinase and renal function in severe cases.

supportResources.name: British Journal of Sports Medicine — Muscle Injuries Resources
supportResources.description: Leading sports medicine journal with open-access muscle injury reviews and consensus statements.

supportResources.name: American Medical Society for Sports Medicine (AMSSM)
supportResources.description: US sports medicine specialty society with patient and clinician resources.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — OrthoInfo
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on muscle injuries, contusions, and rehabilitation.

supportResources.name: World Rugby — Player Welfare
supportResources.description: Practical guidance on contact-sport injury prevention, equipment, and rehabilitation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hamstring Strain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hamstring strain involves indirect eccentric loading during high-speed running and pain is on the back of the thigh. Muscle contusion follows a direct blow, often to the front or side of the thigh, with bruising tracking distally and pain reproduced by direct pressure rather than active stretch.

confusedWithConditions.name: Back Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Back pain is centred on the spine with lumbar tenderness, sometimes radiating down the leg. Muscle contusion is localised to the directly impacted muscle without spine tenderness and follows a clear trauma history.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis causes symmetric polyarthritis with morning stiffness, positive rheumatoid factor, and elevated inflammatory markers. Muscle contusion is a localised post-traumatic soft-tissue injury without joint synovitis or systemic features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcopenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcopenia is age-related muscle wasting in older adults producing generalised weakness over years. Muscle contusion is a localised acute injury after a defined direct blow, with bruising and local tenderness.

relatedConditions.name: Hamstring Strain
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common acute muscle injury in sport; distinguished by mechanism (direct blow vs eccentric overload) but shares rehabilitation principles.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Direct trunk contusions can mimic back pain after collision; mechanism and examination findings distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential for chronic muscle and joint pain when no clear trauma is identified.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Adjacent joint osteoarthritis can be exacerbated by post-contusion altered gait mechanics.

faqs.question: What is a muscle contusion?
faqs.answer: A muscle contusion is a soft-tissue injury caused by a direct blow that crushes muscle fibres and capillaries against underlying bone. It produces a haematoma within the muscle, local pain, swelling, bruising, and restricted joint motion. Quadriceps contusion is the most common type.

faqs.question: How is a muscle contusion different from a strain?
faqs.answer: A contusion results from a direct external blow (knee, ball, helmet). A strain results from indirect tensile overload such as high-speed running or eccentric stretching. Contusions usually bruise visibly along the impact site; strains cause pain on active contraction without a blow.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a muscle contusion?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include immediate pain at the site of impact, swelling within hours, bruising 24-72 hours later, reduced joint motion, pain on resisted contraction, and altered gait. Severe contusions cause marked tenderness, very limited knee flexion, and inability to weight-bear.

faqs.question: How long does it take to heal a muscle contusion?
faqs.answer: Mild contusions resolve in 2-7 days, moderate ones in 7-21 days, and severe ones in 21-42 days. The Jackson and Feagin grading at 12-24 hours after injury (based on passive knee flexion) predicts recovery time for quadriceps contusion.

faqs.question: What is the best initial treatment?
faqs.answer: Follow the PEACE & LOVE framework: rest in the first 1-3 days, elevate, avoid NSAIDs, apply compression, use ice for the first 48 hours, then progressively load the muscle with active range-of-motion and strengthening exercises. Paracetamol is preferred over NSAIDs for pain.

faqs.question: Should I bend my knee or keep it straight?
faqs.answer: For quadriceps contusion, immobilising the knee in 120 degrees of flexion (with an elastic wrap or brace) for the first 24 hours reduces haematoma size and accelerates return-to-play by 4-6 days compared with extension positioning (Aronen et al. 2006).

faqs.question: Should I use ibuprofen?
faqs.answer: Avoid NSAIDs in the first 3-5 days if possible because they may impair early muscle regeneration. Paracetamol is preferred for early pain control. Short courses of NSAIDs can be used later for ongoing pain when paracetamol is insufficient.

faqs.question: Do I need an X-ray or MRI?
faqs.answer: Most mild and moderate contusions do not need imaging. Severe contusions, suspected compartment syndrome, persistent pain or restricted motion at 2-4 weeks, suspected myositis ossificans, and anticoagulated patients with large haematomas should have ultrasound or MRI.

faqs.question: What is myositis ossificans?
faqs.answer: Myositis ossificans traumatica is benign heterotopic bone formation within a muscle haematoma after severe contusion. It develops in 9-17% of severe quadriceps contusions and presents 2-4 weeks later with a palpable lump and reduced range of motion. Most resolve over 3-6 months without surgery.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for a muscle contusion?
faqs.answer: Most muscle contusions do not need surgery. Indications for surgery include acute compartment syndrome (fasciotomy), large symptomatic haematoma (occasional aspiration), and mature symptomatic myositis ossificans (elective excision after 6 months).

faqs.question: What is compartment syndrome?
faqs.answer: Compartment syndrome is a rare but limb-threatening emergency where pressure inside a muscle compartment rises and cuts off perfusion. Symptoms include severe pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia, pallor, pulselessness, and paralysis. Urgent surgical fasciotomy within 6 hours is required.

faqs.question: When can I return to sport?
faqs.answer: Return when pain-free with full range of motion, normal strength on the affected side, and ability to perform sport-specific drills without symptoms. Time-only return is unreliable; objective testing reduces re-injury rates. Continue protective equipment after return.

faqs.question: Can muscle contusions cause long-term problems?
faqs.answer: Most heal completely. Possible long-term issues include myositis ossificans, chronic fibrosis with residual strength deficit, peripheral nerve compression by haematoma, and rare rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury after extensive crush trauma.

faqs.question: Are muscle contusions more common in certain sports?
faqs.answer: Yes. Contact sports — American football, rugby, ice hockey, soccer, lacrosse, martial arts — have incidence of 2-6 per 1,000 player-hours. Non-contact sports have much lower rates. Quadriceps and gluteal contusions are the most common types.

faqs.question: Can I prevent muscle contusions?
faqs.answer: Yes, partly. Wear properly fitted protective equipment, maintain conditioning and agility, use shielding tactics during contact, and enforce safety rules in your sport. Properly fitted thigh and shin pads reduce contusion incidence by 30-50%.

faqs.question: Is heat or ice better for a muscle contusion?
faqs.answer: Use ice (15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours) in the first 48 hours to reduce pain and bleeding. After 3-5 days, switch to heat therapy and gentle stretching to improve range of motion. Avoid heat in the very acute phase as it can increase bleeding.

faqs.question: Are anticoagulants a problem after a muscle contusion?
faqs.answer: Yes. Warfarin, direct oral anticoagulants, and dual antiplatelet therapy increase haematoma size and recovery time after blunt trauma. Patients on anticoagulants should be reviewed promptly after significant contusion; anticoagulation may need temporary adjustment in life-threatening bleeding.

faqs.question: Can I keep playing immediately after a knee-to-thigh blow?
faqs.answer: If pain settles quickly and there is no swelling or weakness, brief continuation may be possible, but worsening pain, swelling, or restricted motion should prompt immediate withdrawal and assessment. The Jackson-Feagin grading at 12-24 hours determines severity.

faqs.question: Should I massage the bruise?
faqs.answer: Deep massage in the first 1-2 weeks is not recommended because it can extend bleeding and increase the risk of myositis ossificans. After 2-3 weeks, gentle soft-tissue work by a physiotherapist may help range of motion and tissue remodelling.

faqs.question: Is a charley horse the same as a contusion?
faqs.answer: The term 'charley horse' is used colloquially in the United States for both muscle cramps and muscle contusions, particularly thigh contusions. Strictly, it more often describes a painful cramp or spasm, but in athletic settings it usually refers to a quadriceps contusion.

faqs.question: Can older adults get muscle contusions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Falls in older adults frequently produce muscle contusions, especially of the thigh and trunk. Reduced subcutaneous fat, anticoagulant use, and slower healing make older patients vulnerable to larger haematomas and slower recovery; multidisciplinary care reduces complications.

metaTitle: Muscle Contusion: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Muscle contusion: bruise of muscle after a direct blow. Grading, treatment with ice and knee flexion, surgery for compartment syndrome and prognosis.

sources.title: Jackson DW, Feagin JA. 1973. Quadriceps contusions in young athletes. Relation of severity of injury to treatment and prognosis. Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery American 55(1):95-105.

Aronen JG, Garrick JG, Chronister RD, McDevitt ER. 2006. Quadriceps contusions: clinical results of immediate immobilization in 120 degrees of knee flexion. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine 16(5):383-387.
Beiner JM, Jokl P. 2002. Muscle contusion injury and myositis ossificans traumatica. Clinical Orthopaedics and Related Research 403(Suppl):S110-S119.
